--- render_with_liquid: false --- Federated Data Science (Technical) — Rhino Health

Federated Data Science: Treatment Response Prediction

Two hospitals — a US oncology network and an EU clinical registry — want to jointly predict which patients will respond to a targeted therapy. HIPAA, GDPR, and institutional governance make pooling raw records off the table.

Federated learning inverts the standard ML pipeline: instead of centralizing data, computation runs inside each institution's own environment and only aggregates or model updates return to the coordinator. This notebook covers the full workflow — cohort analysis, outlier removal, federated XGBoost training via NVIDIA FLARE v2.8, and inference — across 3,588 patients at two geographically separated sites.

All federated calls in this notebook follow the same pattern: a function in rhino_sdk_utils.py wraps a Rhino SDK call, dispatches the computation to one or both hospitals, and returns only the aggregate result. Code at each site runs inside a sandboxed container; this notebook never holds or touches raw patient records directly.

Phase 1 · Project Setup

Authenticate and resolve the project and dataset handles. Each dataset is registered in-place — the connector points at the hospital's existing data system (Snowflake, Databricks, cloud storage) without copying anything.

import rhino_health as rh
from rhino_sdk_utils import *
from op_credentials import get_credentials

PROJECT_NAME = "Oncology Real-World Evidence Study"  # ← update
SITE_US_DATASET = "Dataset A.1"  # ← update
SITE_EU_DATASET = "Dataset B"  # ← update
SITE_US_NAME = "Hospital 1"  # ← update (display name for US hospital)
SITE_EU_NAME = "Hospital 2"  # ← update (display name for EU hospital)
TEST_DATASET = "Dataset B"  # ← update
CODE_OBJECT_NAME = "XGBoost 2.7"  # ← update

USERNAME, PASSWORD = get_credentials("Solutions Env", "Project Lead [Solutions Env]")
session = rh.login(username=USERNAME, rhino_api_url=rh.ApiEnvironment.SOLUTIONS_GCP_URL, password=PASSWORD)

projects = session.project.get_projects()
project = next((p for p in projects if p.name == PROJECT_NAME), None)

print_auth_summary(session, project, username=USERNAME)
Successfully Authenticated to Rhino Platform
User: sam.smith@projectlead.org

Working on Project 'Oncology Real-World Evidence Study'
Project UID: e41363b4-8efb-4df4-8bd3-a27cf6506b18

Load datasets

Datasets are resolved by name and returned as lightweight handles. All subsequent SDK calls reference them by UID; no data is transferred at this step.

hospital_1_data = session.dataset.get_dataset_by_name(SITE_US_DATASET, project_uid=str(project.uid))
hospital_2_data = session.dataset.get_dataset_by_name(SITE_EU_DATASET, project_uid=str(project.uid))

all_uids = [str(hospital_1_data.uid), str(hospital_2_data.uid)]
print_registered_datasets(session, [(hospital_1_data, "US"), (hospital_2_data, "EU")])
Datasets Registered By Collaborators:

Site 1 (Location: US)
Dataset UID: 0d3a9227-e561-46e6-b5c6-956afb964b8d

Site 2 (Location: EU)
Dataset UID: dcda22de-7377-47e6-93f8-03893f9bf94d

Phase 2 · Federated Analytics

Before training, characterize both cohorts. The key constraint: we cannot inspect individual records. The SDK dispatches aggregate queries — counts, means, cross-tabulations — and each hospital returns only the summary. Raw data never leaves either site.

The cohorts differ meaningfully: 1,403 patients at Hospital 1 (US), 2,184 at Hospital 2 (EU), with treatment response rates of 33.0% and 12.3% respectively. The class imbalance and the population shift between sites will both matter for model selection and evaluation.

summary = fetch_cohort_summary(session, hospital_1_data, hospital_2_data, all_uids)
Rhino Server contacting Site 1…
✓  Site 1 — 1,403 patients | Mean Age: 24.8 yrs | Response Rate: 33.0%

Rhino Server contacting Site 2…
✓  Site 2 — 2,184 patients | Mean Age: 30.6 yrs | Response Rate: 12.3%

✓  Cohort summary ready — 3,588 patients across both hospitals | Federated Response Rate: 20.4%

Demographic distributions

Comparing comorbidity burden and time-since-diagnosis across sites. Cohort heterogeneity is expected; a federated model trained on both should generalize better than either single-site model.

k-anonymization: The platform can enforce a minimum count threshold on any aggregate query — buckets with fewer than k patients are suppressed rather than returned, preventing re-identification from small group sizes. Set minimum_count=k in the metric configuration to enable it.

age_a, age_b = fetch_age_bands(session, hospital_1_data, hospital_2_data)
covar_a, covar_b = fetch_covariate_distributions(session, hospital_1_data, hospital_2_data)
plot_demographics(age_a, age_b, covar_a, covar_b)
Rhino Server contacting Site 1 and Site 2 for comorbidity counts…
✓  Comorbidity distribution ready — 6 bands received from Site 1 and Site 2    
Rhino Server contacting Site 1 and Site 2 for years since diagnosis…
✓  Years since diagnosis ready — 7 brackets received from Site 1 and Site 2

Pairwise correlations

Pearson correlations across key clinical features, computed separately per hospital and displayed side by side. Features with divergent correlation structure between sites are candidates for interaction terms or site-stratified preprocessing.

corr_a = build_corr_matrix(session, str(hospital_1_data.uid), CORR_VARS, "US Hospital")
corr_b = build_corr_matrix(session, str(hospital_2_data.uid), CORR_VARS, "EU Hospital")
plot_correlation_heatmaps(corr_a, corr_b, CORR_LABELS)
✓  Correlation matrix ready for US Hospital — 28 variable pairs                         
✓  Correlation matrix ready for EU Hospital — 28 variable pairs

Phase 3 · Model Training

Federated outlier removal

Standard z-score outlier removal requires global feature statistics (μ, σ). Computing these centrally is impossible here — neither site can see the other's records. Instead, each hospital reports its per-feature mean and variance; the coordinator combines them via the pooled-variance formula to get global μ and σ, then ships the resulting bounds back. Each site applies the same 3σ cutoff locally.

TEST_FRACTION = 0.2

bounds = compute_outlier_bounds(session, all_uids, NUMERIC_FEATURES, zscore_threshold=3.0)
display_outlier_bounds(bounds)
✓  Outlier bounds ready — 7 features computed jointly across both hospitals
Federated 3σ Outlier Bounds — aggregated across both hospitals
  Lower Bound (μ − 3σ) Upper Bound (μ + 3σ)
Clinical Feature    
patient_age 10.95 45.67
baseline_tumor_marker -73,869.83 216,446.65
years_since_diagnosis -11.78 21.81
tumor_size_mm -10,555.88 32,680.15
pdl1_expression_pct 0.90 21.49
ctdna_vaf -0.34 0.78
months_on_prior_therapy -16.65 39.31

Train / test split

20% stratified holdout, stratified on the outcome label. Given the class imbalance (~33% / ~12% response), an unstratified split would produce inconsistent positive rates across the train and test sets and inflate variance in threshold-based metrics.

data_prep_code = build_data_prep_snippet(bounds, TEST_FRACTION, column_rename=COLUMN_RENAME)
deploy_and_run_snippet(
    session,
    project,
    "Outlier Removal & Test/Train Split",
    data_prep_code,
    datasets=[hospital_1_data, hospital_2_data],
    output_templates=[
        [f"{SITE_US_DATASET} - Train", f"{SITE_US_DATASET} - Test"],
        [f"{SITE_EU_DATASET} - Train", f"{SITE_EU_DATASET} - Test"],
    ],
    requirements=["pandas", "scikit-learn"],
)

hospital_1_train, hospital_2_train, hospital_1_test, hospital_2_test = fetch_datasets(
    session,
    project,
    [
        f"{SITE_US_DATASET} - Train",
        f"{SITE_EU_DATASET} - Train",
        f"{SITE_US_DATASET} - Test",
        f"{SITE_EU_DATASET} - Test",
    ],
)
train_uids = [str(hospital_1_train.uid), str(hospital_2_train.uid)]
test_uids = [str(hospital_1_test.uid), str(hospital_2_test.uid)]
print("Train/test split complete ✓")
Rhino Server deploying 'Outlier Removal & Test/Train Split' to both hospitals…
  Running at Project Lead… (1/2)
  Running at Site 2… (2/2)
✓  Data preparation complete — cleaned datasets registered at both hospitals

Federated XGBoost via NVIDIA FLARE v2.8

FLARE coordinates training using histogram-based gradient aggregation: each site computes local gradient histograms over its data; the server aggregates them to find globally optimal split points; and the updated tree is broadcast back. Raw gradients and patient-level data never leave the site.

Three models are trained under identical hyperparameters to isolate the effect of federation:

Model Training data
Hospital 1 model US cohort only
Hospital 2 model EU cohort only
Global model Both cohorts, federated 
import json

train_matrix = {
    "Hospital 1 Model": [hospital_1_train.uid],
    "Hospital 2 Model": [hospital_2_train.uid],
    "Global Model": [hospital_1_train.uid, hospital_2_train.uid],
}

with open("xgboost_flare/app/config/config_fed_client.json") as f:
    client_config = json.load(f)
with open("xgboost_flare/app/config/config_fed_server.json") as f:
    server_config = json.load(f)

code_runs = run_federated_training(
    session, project, CODE_OBJECT_NAME, train_matrix, client_config, server_config
)
print("All training runs complete ✓")
Rhino Server dispatching 3 training run(s) across federated sites…
  Waiting for 'Hospital 1 Model' to complete… [1/3]
  Waiting for 'Hospital 2 Model' to complete… [2/3]
  Waiting for 'Global Model' to complete… [3/3]
✓  All 3 training run(s) complete — model weights aggregated by Rhino Server
All training runs complete ✓

Evaluation

Inference runs locally at each hospital; only the resulting prediction scores are returned. We evaluate on the held-out test sets using two complementary views: the ROC curve (threshold-free discrimination) and a fixed-threshold breakdown at 0.5.

test_matrix = {
    "Hospital 1 Test Set": [hospital_1_test.uid],
    "Hospital 2 Test Set": [hospital_2_test.uid],
}

inference_results = run_inference_matrix(session, code_runs, test_matrix)
  Rhino Server sending 'Hospital 1 Model' to Hospital 1 Test Set for inference… [1/4]
  Rhino Server sending 'Hospital 2 Model' to Hospital 2 Test Set for inference… [2/4]
  Rhino Server sending 'Global Model' to Hospital 1 Test Set for inference… [3/4]
  Rhino Server sending 'Global Model' to Hospital 2 Test Set for inference… [4/4]
✓  All 4 inference run(s) complete — predictions generated locally at each hospital

metrics = compute_evaluation_metrics(session, inference_results)

plot_roc_curves(metrics)
Rhino Server computing evaluation metrics at each hospital…
  Evaluating 'Hospital 1 Model' across both hospitals… [1/3]
  Evaluating 'Hospital 2 Model' across both hospitals… [2/3]
  Evaluating 'Global Model' across both hospitals… [3/3]
✓  Evaluation metrics ready — 3 model(s) scored, no patient data left either site

Fixed-threshold metrics (threshold = 0.5)

Five metrics across the three models. Given the class imbalance, accuracy is uninformative — these measures are more appropriate:

Metric What it measures
Average Precision Area under the precision-recall curve; sensitive to the positive class, useful under imbalance.
F1 Harmonic mean of precision and recall at the decision threshold.
F2 Weights recall twice as heavily as precision — appropriate when false negatives are costlier than false positives.
Balanced Accuracy Mean of sensitivity and specificity; equivalent to macro-averaged recall.
MCC Matthews Correlation Coefficient — accounts for all four confusion matrix cells, ranges −1 to +1. Considered more informative than F1 on imbalanced datasets.

Reporting all five guards against metric cherry-picking and reveals any precision/recall tradeoff the global model may be making.

plot_scalar_metrics_and_confusion(metrics)

Phase 4 · Inference

The trained global model is packaged into a self-contained container and deployed to each hospital. Scoring runs locally: the container accepts a patient dataset (without the outcome label), applies the model, and returns a scores column (0–1 response probability) and a predicted_class column (hard label at 0.5).

inference_container = create_inference_container(
    session, project, code_runs["Global Model"], container_path="inference_container/"
)
Extracting trained model weights from the Global Model…

Waiting for code object build to complete (0 hours a minute and 2 seconds)
Done.
✓  Inference container ready — model packaged and registered for deployment to both hospitals

inference_result = run_production_inference(
    session, inference_container, test_datasets=[hospital_1_test, hospital_2_test]
)
Rhino Server deploying inference container to 2 site(s) — predictions will run locally…
Waiting for code run to complete (0 hours 0 minutes and a second)
Waiting for code run to complete (0 hours 0 minutes and 11 seconds)
Waiting for code run to complete (0 hours 0 minutes and 21 seconds)
Waiting for code run to complete (0 hours 0 minutes and 32 seconds)
Done.
✓  Production inference complete — scored datasets created at 2 site(s), no data left the institution

Results

The global federated model outperforms both single-site models on both test sets:

Model AUC @ US test AUC @ EU test
Single-site models 0.802 0.819
Global (federated) 0.871 0.884

The gap holds across all five threshold-based metrics. The global model benefits from the broader population distribution — the two cohorts have meaningfully different response rates (33% vs 12%), and jointly training on both reduces the bias each single-site model inherits from its own population.
